stem: A 24-year-old woman gravida 1 at 8 weeks gestation by certain dates presents to your emergency department after five days of inability to tolerate oral fluids with vomiting more than ten times daily. She has lost 4.2 kg from her booking weight three weeks ago and reports dizziness and orthostatic symptoms. Examination shows dry mucous membranes and tachycardia. Her urine ketones are 3+ and serum sodium is 132 mmol/L. Routine antenatal bloods include normal TSH, normal LFTs and a viable intrauterine pregnancy on bedside ultrasound. She has had no improvement on oral pyridoxine and doxylamine over three days.

data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes; postural BP drop 18 mmHg; weight loss 4.2 kg (5.6%)
data_table.Investigations: Urine ketones 3+; Na 132, K 3.4, Cr 76; TSH 1.2; viable 8/40 IUP on USS
data_table.Medications: Doxylamine 25 mg + pyridoxine 25 mg nocte (3 days, no relief)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Admit for intravenous Hartmann's resuscitation, intravenous metoclopramide 10 mg eight-hourly and add intravenous thiamine 100 mg before any dextrose-containing fluids
options.rationale: Severe hyperemesis with ketonuria, weight loss and orthostasis requires admission for parenteral rehydration and antiemetic escalation; thiamine before dextrose prevents Wernicke encephalopathy in protracted vomiting. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023

options.text: Add ondansetron 8 mg oral wafer eight-hourly to her current oral antiemetics and discharge home with general practice follow-up scheduled within 48 hours
options.rationale: Severe hyperemesis with ketonuria and over 5% weight loss requires admission rather than further oral therapy at home; oral wafers will not be tolerated in active vomiting. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023

options.text: Commence intravenous 5% dextrose with potassium replacement before any thiamine to provide immediate caloric and electrolyte support in starvation ketosis
options.rationale: Dextrose administration before thiamine in protracted vomiting risks precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy; thiamine must precede any dextrose-containing fluid. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023

options.text: Commence corticosteroid intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg six-hourly as first-line antiemetic for refractory hyperemesis at her current gestation today
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are reserved for refractory hyperemesis after failure of multiple antiemetics, and the timing within the first trimester is more cautious due to a small cleft palate signal. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023

options.text: Order an urgent ultrasound to exclude gestational trophoblastic disease as the cause of severe vomiting before any pharmacological escalation in management is undertaken
options.rationale: Her bedside ultrasound has already confirmed a viable singleton intrauterine pregnancy; resuscitation and antiemetic escalation should not be delayed. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum is diagnosed by persistent vomiting plus over 5% pre-pregnancy weight loss, ketonuria and dehydration. Management requires admission for crystalloid rehydration and parenteral antiemetic. Thiamine 100 mg must precede any dextrose-containing infusion in patients with protracted vomiting to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. First-line parenteral antiemetics include metoclopramide and prochlorperazine; ondansetron is added or used early when other agents fail. Corticosteroids are reserved for refractory cases.
key_points: HG diagnosis: persistent vomiting, >5% weight loss, ketonuria, dehydration
Admit for IV rehydration and parenteral antiemetics when severe
Thiamine 100 mg before dextrose to prevent Wernicke
Corticosteroids reserved for refractory cases
explanation.pearls: When a pregnant patient with hyperemesis requires intravenous fluids, give intravenous thiamine 100 mg before any dextrose-containing fluid and start metoclopramide 10 mg every 8 hours rather than escalating oral therapy.

sources.label: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy and Hyperemesis Gravidarum 2023
Therapeutic Guidelines Gastrointestinal: Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy 2024

stem: A 28-year-old woman gravida 2 para 1 attends your early pregnancy assessment clinic with mild right iliac fossa pain and a serum beta-hCG of 2400 IU/L two days ago that has now risen to 2860 IU/L. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with no intrauterine sac, a 22 mm right adnexal mass with no fetal heart activity and no free fluid. She is haemodynamically stable, has no pain currently, and her renal and liver function are normal. She has no contraindication to methotrexate and is keen to avoid surgery if possible. Her partner can drive her in for serial reviews easily.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen now; mild right iliac fossa tenderness on deep palpation
data_table.Investigations: bHCG 2860 (was 2400 over 2 days); 22 mm right adnexal mass, no FH, no free fluid; Hb 128; LFTs normal; eGFR 92

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management option to offer her?

options.text: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG monitoring every 48 hours given mild symptoms and a stable haemodynamic profile with reassuring ultrasound findings today
options.rationale: Expectant management requires plateauing or falling beta-hCG, no symptoms and an adnexal mass under 30 mm; her beta-hCG is rising and she has symptoms, so expectant management is inappropriate. (Source: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023

options.text: Single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg per square metre with weekly serum beta-hCG measurement on days 4 and 7 then weekly to non-pregnant
options.rationale: She meets eligibility for single-dose methotrexate: haemodynamic stability, beta-hCG below 5000 IU/L, no fetal heart activity, no significant free fluid, normal renal and liver function, and reliable follow-up. (Source: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingectomy as first-line definitive management given any tubal ectopic carries an unacceptable risk of rupture with conservative non-surgical approaches
options.rationale: Medical management is appropriate first-line for eligible women and avoids loss of the tube; surgery is reserved for those who do not meet methotrexate criteria, who fail medical therapy or who have ruptured. (Source: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingostomy with conservation of the affected tube and intraoperative methotrexate instillation to reduce persistent trophoblast risk after surgery completes
options.rationale: Salpingostomy is generally inferior to salpingectomy when the contralateral tube is healthy; methotrexate instillation is not routine intraoperative practice. (Source: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023

options.text: Oral mifepristone 200 mg followed by misoprostol 800 micrograms vaginally as a non-surgical alternative for tubal ectopic pregnancy with rapid effective resolution today
options.rationale: Mifepristone and misoprostol are used for intrauterine pregnancy termination, not ectopic pregnancy; they do not resolve tubal trophoblast. (Source: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023

explanation.summary: Methotrexate is appropriate for haemodynamically stable women with confirmed unruptured ectopic pregnancy, beta-hCG below 5000 IU/L, no fetal heart activity, an adnexal mass under 35 mm, no significant free fluid, normal renal and liver function and reliable follow-up. Single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg per square metre is given with day-4 and day-7 beta-hCG measurement; a fall of 15% or more between days 4 and 7 indicates response, otherwise a second dose or surgery is required. Patients require pregnancy-prevention counselling for at least three months post-treatment due to methotrexate teratogenicity.
key_points: Eligibility: stable, bHCG <5000, no FH, mass <35 mm, no significant free fluid, normal renal and liver function
Single dose 50 mg per square metre intramuscularly
Day 4 to day 7 fall of >=15% indicates response
Pregnancy prevention for at least 3 months post-treatment
explanation.pearls: Offer methotrexate for unruptured ectopic with beta-hCG <5000 IU/L, no fetal heart and mass <35 mm; check day-4 and day-7 beta-hCG and give a second dose if the day 4 to day 7 fall is under 15%.

sources.label: RANZCOG Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy 2023

stem: A 32-year-old woman gravida 3 para 2 at 35 weeks and 4 days gestation is admitted to your antenatal ward with a small painless fresh red vaginal bleed that has now ceased. Her 32-week placental localisation transabdominal scan and confirmatory transvaginal scan showed a placenta praevia with its lower edge covering the internal cervical os. She has had two previous caesarean sections. Her bleeding has settled, the fetus is reactive on cardiotocography and her observations remain stable. The Hb has dropped from 116 to 109 g per litre since admission and she remains rhesus positive.

data_table.Examination: Soft uterus; no tenderness; fundal height 36 cm; CTG reactive
data_table.Investigations: Hb 109 (admission 116); blood group O Rh+; placenta covering os on TVS 32/40; FH 152 reactive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive plan for this pregnancy?

options.text: Proceed to immediate emergency caesarean tonight because of the antepartum haemorrhage event despite cessation of bleeding and reassuring fetal heart rate tracing
options.rationale: With cessation of bleeding, stable observations and reactive cardiotocography, expectant inpatient management with planned delivery at 36 to 37 weeks reduces neonatal morbidity compared with immediate preterm delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023

options.text: Plan vaginal trial of labour at 39 weeks given resolution of bleeding and confidence that the placental edge will retract upward with further uterine growth before labour
options.rationale: Major placenta praevia where the placenta covers the os is an absolute contraindication to vaginal delivery regardless of bleeding pattern; the placental edge does not retract sufficiently with continued growth to allow safe vaginal birth. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023

options.text: Plan elective caesarean section at 36 to 37 weeks after antenatal corticosteroids, with imaging review for placenta accreta spectrum due to prior caesareans
options.rationale: Major placenta praevia with prior caesareans warrants planned caesarean at 36 to 37 weeks after a course of antenatal corticosteroids, with active assessment for placenta accreta spectrum given the major risk factors. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023

options.text: Plan induction of labour at 38 weeks with continuous cardiotocography and full neonatal team in attendance in an obstetric theatre with surgical backup standing ready
options.rationale: Induction of labour is contraindicated for major placenta praevia; planned caesarean is the only appropriate mode of delivery in this scenario. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023

options.text: Discharge home with daily fetal movement monitoring and return precautions; plan caesarean at 39 weeks if no further bleeding events occur over the following weeks
options.rationale: Inpatient observation after an antepartum haemorrhage with placenta praevia is recommended until delivery; outpatient management is reserved for highly selected stable women with rapid hospital access. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023

explanation.summary: Major placenta praevia (placenta covering the internal os) is a contraindication to vaginal birth. Planned caesarean delivery is recommended at 36 to 37 weeks after a course of antenatal corticosteroids in stable women. After an antepartum bleed, inpatient management until delivery is generally recommended. Prior caesarean section combined with placenta praevia substantially raises the risk of placenta accreta spectrum and warrants additional imaging (specialised ultrasound or MRI), multidisciplinary planning and delivery in a centre with interventional radiology and blood bank capacity.
key_points: Major praevia: planned caesarean at 36 to 37 weeks
Antenatal corticosteroids before preterm caesarean
Prior caesarean + praevia = placenta accreta spectrum risk; image specifically
Inpatient observation after antepartum haemorrhage until delivery
explanation.pearls: When a woman with major placenta praevia and prior caesareans has an antepartum bleed at 35 weeks, plan caesarean at 36 to 37 weeks after steroids and arrange targeted placenta accreta spectrum imaging rather than aiming for term or attempting vaginal trial.

sources.label: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta Spectrum 2023

stem: A 27-year-old woman gravida 2 para 1 at 31 weeks and 2 days gestation presents to your birth suite with a sudden gush of clear fluid two hours ago that continues to leak. Speculum examination confirms pooling of amniotic fluid and a closed cervix. The fetus is in cephalic presentation with reassuring cardiotocography. She is afebrile with no abdominal tenderness, fetal heart rate 142 with normal variability, and no contractions. Routine bloods show WCC 9.4 and CRP 8. Her group B Streptococcus status from a recent swab is negative. She has no penicillin allergy or relevant medical history.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: pooling of clear amniotic fluid; closed cervix; non-tender uterus
data_table.Investigations: WCC 9.4; CRP 8; GBS negative; CTG 142 reactive; vaginal swab pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Discharge home with daily community midwifery review, oral amoxicillin and salbutamol nebulisers as needed for any contractions starting in the next 48 hours
options.rationale: PPROM requires inpatient management for monitoring of chorioamnionitis and fetal status; outpatient management and amoxicillin alone are inappropriate. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023

options.text: Admit, give betamethasone and intravenous co-amoxiclav 1.2 g eight-hourly for 10 days for combined gram-positive and gram-negative coverage during latency observation today
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav is contraindicated in PPROM because of a documented association with neonatal necrotising enterocolitis; erythromycin is the recommended latency antibiotic. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023

options.text: Induce labour now with vaginal prostaglandin and intravenous oxytocin given preterm prelabour rupture of membranes carries an unacceptable ongoing chorioamnionitis risk
options.rationale: Expectant management at 31 weeks until 34 weeks is recommended when there is no chorioamnionitis, fetal distress or other indication for delivery; immediate induction increases neonatal morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023

options.text: Admit, give betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscular for two doses 24 hours apart, oral erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days and avoid digital examinations completely
options.rationale: PPROM management at 31 weeks includes admission, antenatal corticosteroids, latency antibiotics with erythromycin (no co-amoxiclav due to necrotising enterocolitis risk) and avoidance of digital examinations to reduce ascending infection. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023

options.text: Perform a digital vaginal examination now to assess cervical change in centimetres and add a single 6 g dose of magnesium sulfate intravenously for fetal lung neuroprotection
options.rationale: Digital examination is avoided in PPROM as it accelerates ascending infection; magnesium sulfate neuroprotection is indicated only when preterm birth is imminent within 24 hours. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023

explanation.summary: PPROM at 31 weeks is managed with admission, antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscularly 24 hours apart for two doses), latency antibiotics (erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days; co-amoxiclav avoided due to neonatal necrotising enterocolitis risk) and avoidance of digital vaginal examination to reduce ascending infection. Expectant management is continued until 34 weeks unless chorioamnionitis, fetal distress or other complications develop. Magnesium sulfate fetal neuroprotection is given when preterm birth is imminent.
key_points: Erythromycin is the latency antibiotic; co-amoxiclav contraindicated
Avoid digital examination
Antenatal corticosteroids 24 hours apart
Expectant management to 34 weeks unless complications develop
explanation.pearls: For PPROM under 34 weeks without chorioamnionitis or fetal distress, give betamethasone plus erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days and avoid co-amoxiclav and digital examinations, planning delivery at 34 weeks if no complications arise.

sources.label: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes 2023

stem: A 31-year-old woman gravida 1 at 19 weeks gestation with a monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy attends your obstetric ultrasound clinic for routine fortnightly surveillance scanning. The scan shows twin A with an empty bladder visualised throughout the scan, deepest vertical pool of amniotic fluid 1.4 cm, and small bladder. Twin B has deepest vertical pool 9.8 cm, distended bladder, and visible cardiac dilatation. Both fetuses have normal umbilical artery Doppler waveforms at this time. Maternal blood pressure and observations are normal. There are no maternal symptoms of preterm labour.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus; fundal height 24 cm
data_table.Investigations: Twin A DVP 1.4 cm; Twin B DVP 9.8 cm with distended bladder; UA Dopplers normal both

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Plan elective delivery within 48 hours to remove both twins from the abnormal placental environment causing the syndrome with neonatal intensive care for the preterm pair
options.rationale: Delivery at 19 weeks is non-viable and is not an appropriate management option; in-utero laser treatment is the standard of care. (Source: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023

options.text: Continue fortnightly surveillance unchanged and reassess fluid discrepancy and bladder visibility in two weeks before deciding on any escalation in monitoring or referral
options.rationale: TTTS at Quintero stage II with absent donor bladder cannot wait two weeks; delay risks progression to Quintero stage III to V and significant loss in fetal salvage rates. (Source: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023

options.text: Perform a single therapeutic amnioreduction of 1.5 litres from the recipient sac today as first-line definitive treatment without further specialist fetal medicine referral
options.rationale: Amnioreduction has worse long-term twin survival and neurodevelopmental outcomes than laser ablation and should only be considered when laser is unavailable or contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023

options.text: Recommend selective fetal reduction of the smaller twin by bipolar cord coagulation to preserve outcomes for the recipient larger twin in this monochorionic twin scenario
options.rationale: Selective reduction is reserved for late or severe TTTS where one twin is severely compromised and laser is not feasible; it is not first-line for Quintero stage II TTTS at 19 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023

options.text: Refer urgently to a fetal medicine centre for evaluation and consideration of fetoscopic laser ablation of communicating placental anastomoses as treatment of choice now
options.rationale: Quintero stage I or higher twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome diagnosed before 26 weeks is best treated with fetoscopic laser ablation, which has superior twin survival outcomes compared with serial amnioreduction; referral is time-critical. (Source: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023

explanation.summary: Twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome complicates around 10 to 15% of monochorionic twin pregnancies. Diagnosis requires monochorionicity plus oligohydramnios (deepest vertical pool below 2 cm) in the donor twin and polyhydramnios (deepest vertical pool above 8 cm before 20 weeks, 10 cm after) in the recipient. Quintero staging incorporates bladder visibility, Doppler abnormalities, fetal hydrops and demise. Fetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses is the standard of care for Quintero stage II or higher TTTS diagnosed before 26 weeks, with substantially superior twin survival compared with amnioreduction.
key_points: TTTS: oligo (DVP <2 cm) + poly (DVP >8 cm before 20 weeks) in MC twins
Quintero II = absent donor bladder
Fetoscopic laser ablation is the standard of care before 26 weeks
Laser is superior to serial amnioreduction for twin survival and neurodevelopment
explanation.pearls: When a monochorionic twin pregnancy shows DVP <2 cm in one twin and DVP >8 cm in the other before 20 weeks, refer to a fetal medicine centre for fetoscopic laser ablation within 24 to 48 hours rather than continuing fortnightly surveillance or attempting amnioreduction first.

sources.label: RANZCOG Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy 2023

stem: A 2-year-old previously well boy presents to your emergency department with one day of barking cough, hoarse voice and now severe inspiratory stridor at rest with marked sternal and intercostal recession. He is alert but anxious. The triage nurse reports oxygen saturations 93% on room air. He had a low-grade fever the day before. His vaccinations are up to date. He has no drooling, has been drinking small volumes, and there is no neck swelling. The nurse asks what treatment to give immediately at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, BP not done, RR 42, SpO2 93% on RA, Temp 37.4
data_table.Examination: Stridor at rest; marked recession; anxious; mild reduction in air entry both fields
data_table.Investigations: Westley score 8 (severe); clinical diagnosis; no bloods or imaging required

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate initial management?

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 with concurrent single oral dose of dexamethasone 0.6 mg per kilogram while preparing escalation pathway with anaesthetics
options.rationale: Severe croup with stridor at rest and recession warrants immediate nebulised adrenaline for rapid relief plus oral dexamethasone for sustained anti-inflammatory effect, with anaesthetic escalation pathway prepared. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Croup

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg per kilogram once and clinical observation for two hours without nebulised adrenaline because of stimulation and possible distress on application
options.rationale: In severe croup with stridor at rest, nebulised adrenaline is required for rapid airway relief; prednisolone alone is acceptable in mild croup but not for severe presentations. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Croup

options.text: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg per kilogram now for presumed epiglottitis and call ENT for direct laryngoscopy in the operating theatre without delay regardless of vaccination status
options.rationale: Croup, not epiglottitis, is the working diagnosis given barking cough, hoarse voice and stridor; Hib-vaccinated child without drooling makes epiglottitis very unlikely and antibiotics are not first-line therapy here. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Croup

options.text: Heliox 70 of 30 with concurrent nebulised salbutamol as first-line therapy for severe upper airway obstruction in this preschool child without prior wheeze history
options.rationale: Salbutamol is for lower airway bronchospasm and has no role in croup; heliox is at most an adjunct after first-line adrenaline and corticosteroid have been administered. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Croup

options.text: Reassure parents and discharge home with humidified air at home as evidence-based first-line therapy for moderate to severe croup with safety-net advice about deterioration
options.rationale: Humidified air has no evidence of benefit in croup; severe presentations require admission, corticosteroid and adrenaline, not discharge home. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Croup

explanation.summary: Croup is a clinical diagnosis based on barking cough, hoarse voice and inspiratory stridor. Severity is assessed using the Westley score or by clinical features such as stridor at rest, recession, oxygen saturation and conscious state. All severities receive a single dose of oral or intravenous dexamethasone 0.6 mg per kilogram (or prednisolone 1 mg per kilogram in mild cases). Severe croup with stridor at rest, significant recession, or hypoxia requires nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 for rapid relief. Recurrence after adrenaline within 4 hours warrants admission for observation.
key_points: Croup is a clinical diagnosis; bloods and imaging not required
All severities get corticosteroid (dexamethasone 0.6 mg per kilogram is standard)
Severe croup adds nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000
Observe for at least 4 hours after adrenaline for rebound
explanation.pearls: For severe croup with stridor at rest in a child under five, give nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 immediately plus single dose oral or intravenous dexamethasone 0.6 mg per kilogram and observe for 4 hours before discharge consideration.

subtopic_detail: Febrile UTI in infants under 3 months
stem: A 6-week-old infant girl is brought to your emergency department by her parents because of a tactile fever today, feeding refusal and increased sleepiness. She has not had any cough, coryza or rash. She was born at term, has been growing normally on the 50th centile, and was vaccinated at 6 weeks two days ago. On examination she has a temperature of 38.4 degrees Celsius rectally, is mildly irritable when handled and has no obvious focus of infection on careful examination including ears, throat and chest.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, BP 86/54, RR 48, SpO2 98% on RA, Temp 38.4 rectal
data_table.Examination: Mildly irritable; AF flat; no rash; no respiratory distress; no focus on examination
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.4; CRP 38; venous pH 7.36, lactate 2.4; urine pending; chest X-ray clear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Send a bag urine sample for culture and discharge home with safety-net advice and paediatric follow-up at the local general practice clinic in 24 hours
options.rationale: Febrile infants under 3 months are at high risk of bacteraemia and meningitis and require admission and full septic workup; bag urine has high contamination rates and is not used. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

options.text: Admit, take blood cultures, lumbar puncture and a clean catch or suprapubic aspirate urine, then commence intravenous benzylpenicillin plus cefotaxime empirically as a septic workup approach
options.rationale: Febrile infants under 3 months require full septic workup including blood cultures, lumbar puncture and catheter or suprapubic aspirate urine, with immediate empirical intravenous antibiotics pending cultures regardless of clinical appearance. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

options.text: Reassure family that her recent vaccination explains the fever and observe for 4 hours without bloods, urine, lumbar puncture or empirical antibiotic therapy at the present time
options.rationale: Vaccine-associated fever does not explain fever above 38 degrees in a 6-week-old; serious bacterial infection must be excluded before attribution to vaccination. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

options.text: Perform a urinalysis only and treat as a presumed urinary tract infection with oral cefalexin for ten days while awaiting culture confirmation from the bag sample collected today
options.rationale: Empirical oral antibiotics for an infant under 3 months without lumbar puncture and blood cultures risks missing meningitis or bacteraemia; intravenous antibiotics in hospital are required. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

options.text: Order a chest X-ray and respiratory viral PCR only to identify the likely viral cause of the fever before deciding on the need for any antibiotic therapy in this infant
options.rationale: Identifying a viral cause does not eliminate the risk of serious bacterial infection in this age group; full septic workup and empirical antibiotics are still required. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

explanation.summary: All febrile infants under 3 months require admission for full septic workup including blood culture, lumbar puncture and catheter or suprapubic aspirate urine, with empirical intravenous antibiotic cover for likely pathogens (commonly benzylpenicillin plus cefotaxime, plus aciclovir if HSV is suspected). Bag urine is unsuitable for diagnosis because of contamination. UTI is the single most common serious bacterial infection in this age group and meningitis must be actively excluded. Risk stratification (Rochester, Step-by-Step) may be applied to selected well-appearing infants but is not used to defer workup at this age.
key_points: All febrile infants under 3 months: admit, full septic workup, empirical IV antibiotics
Catheter or suprapubic aspirate urine; bag urine not used
Lumbar puncture mandatory before empirical antibiotics where feasible
Empirical cover: benzylpenicillin plus cefotaxime
explanation.pearls: For any febrile (>=38.0) infant under 3 months, admit and complete blood culture plus lumbar puncture plus clean-catch or suprapubic aspirate urine before commencing empirical intravenous benzylpenicillin plus cefotaxime rather than discharging on oral antibiotics.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant Under 3 Months

stem: A 3-year-old boy presents to your emergency department on day 6 of an illness characterised by persistent fever above 38.5 degrees Celsius. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue with cracked red lips, a polymorphous truncal rash, oedema of the hands and feet and a unilateral 2.5 cm cervical lymph node. He is irritable and his bloods show CRP 124 mg per litre, WCC 18.4, platelets 540, ALT 78 and a normocytic anaemia. Urinalysis shows sterile pyuria. Echocardiography has been requested and will occur within hours.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral conjunctival injection; strawberry tongue; cracked lips; polymorphous rash; oedema of hands and feet; 2.5 cm right cervical node
data_table.Investigations: CRP 124; WCC 18.4 with neutrophilia; platelets 540; ALT 78; Hb 96 (microcytic); urine sterile pyuria

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Wait until day 10 of illness to confirm coronary artery aneurysm on echocardiography before commencing intravenous immunoglobulin therapy and aspirin antiplatelet therapy together
options.rationale: Delaying treatment until day 10 to await coronary artery aneurysm formation forfeits the window in which IVIG prevents aneurysms; treatment is recommended within 10 days of fever onset on clinical criteria. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease

options.text: Commence intravenous flucloxacillin and ceftriaxone for presumed streptococcal toxic shock syndrome while awaiting blood cultures and discontinue if cultures are negative at 48 hours
options.rationale: Streptococcal toxic shock syndrome presents with shock and a desquamating rash rather than the constellation of features here; failure to give IVIG within 10 days of fever onset increases coronary aneurysm risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g per kilogram over 10 to 12 hours plus aspirin 30 to 50 mg per kilogram per day in four divided doses initially in hospital
options.rationale: Kawasaki disease meeting at least five of five principal features warrants intravenous immunoglobulin within 10 days of fever onset to reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk, plus high-dose aspirin until afebrile and CRP normalises. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease

options.text: Treat with high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg per kilogram daily for three days as first-line monotherapy without intravenous immunoglobulin in routine Kawasaki disease management
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are an adjunct for IVIG-resistant disease or high-risk Kawasaki, not a substitute for IVIG as first-line therapy in standard presentations. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease

options.text: Discharge with oral ibuprofen and paediatric outpatient review in three days if the fever persists beyond day 8 of illness with no other planned investigations or interventions today
options.rationale: Discharge of suspected Kawasaki disease delays IVIG and risks coronary aneurysm formation; admission and treatment within 10 days of fever onset is the standard of care. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a clinical diagnosis requiring fever for 5 or more days plus 4 of 5 principal features (bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, oral mucosal changes, polymorphous rash, extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy at least 1.5 cm). Untreated disease leads to coronary artery aneurysm in around 25% of children. Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g per kilogram over 10 to 12 hours within 10 days of fever onset reduces aneurysm risk to under 5%. High-dose aspirin is given until afebrile and inflammatory markers normalise, then continued at low antiplatelet dose for 6 to 8 weeks pending echocardiogram. Corticosteroids are added in high-risk or IVIG-resistant disease.
key_points: Clinical diagnosis: fever >=5 days plus 4 of 5 principal features
IVIG 2 g per kilogram within 10 days of fever onset
Aspirin 30 to 50 mg per kilogram per day until afebrile, then low-dose
Echocardiography at diagnosis, 2 weeks and 6 to 8 weeks
explanation.pearls: When fever has lasted 5 or more days with 4 of 5 Kawasaki features, give IVIG 2 g per kilogram plus aspirin within day 10 of fever onset rather than awaiting coronary aneurysm formation; this reduces aneurysm risk from 25% to under 5%.

stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to your paediatric clinic for follow-up of IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schonlein purpura) diagnosed three weeks ago by characteristic palpable purpura over the buttocks and lower limbs with mild abdominal pain. He has had no melaena, intussusception or arthritis. The previous review four days ago was unremarkable. Today his mother reports brown-coloured urine in the morning, ankle swelling and a blood pressure recorded at the local pharmacy as 132/86 mmHg. His urinalysis in your rooms shows haematuria 3+, proteinuria 3+ and red cell casts.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 134/88 (>95th centile), RR 18, SpO2 99%, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Faded purpura on lower limbs; mild bilateral ankle oedema
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis blood 3+, protein 3+; RBC casts; Cr 78 (was 38); albumin 28; urine PCR 380 mg/mmol

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase fluid intake and recheck urinalysis and renal function in two weeks with attendance at general practice if proteinuria worsens or new symptoms develop with no other intervention today
options.rationale: Severe HSP nephritis with hypertension and rising creatinine cannot wait two weeks for review; urgent nephrology referral is essential to prevent permanent renal injury. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura

options.text: Reassure family that haematuria after IgA vasculitis is universal and reviewing the urinalysis in three months is sufficient management without escalation of investigations today
options.rationale: Microscopic haematuria alone may be observed, but nephrotic-range proteinuria, hypertension and rising creatinine reflect severe nephritis requiring urgent specialist involvement. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg per kilogram per day at the next routine general practice visit without specialist nephrology input or escalation of investigations today
options.rationale: Empirical corticosteroids without specialist guidance are inadequate for severe HSP nephritis; biopsy guides selection of pulsed corticosteroid plus other immunosuppressive agents. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura

options.text: Refer urgently to a paediatric nephrologist for renal biopsy assessment and consideration of immunosuppressive therapy alongside renin-angiotensin system blockade for proteinuria
options.rationale: HSP nephritis with nephrotic-range proteinuria, hypertension and rising creatinine constitutes severe nephritis warranting paediatric nephrology referral, biopsy consideration and immunosuppressive therapy in selected cases. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura

options.text: Commence intravenous cyclophosphamide 750 mg per square metre as outpatient therapy in your general practice rooms today for the severe IgA vasculitis nephritic flare currently
options.rationale: Cyclophosphamide is an immunosuppressive option in crescentic HSP nephritis but only under specialist nephrology supervision after biopsy; it is not appropriate to commence in general practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura

explanation.summary: IgA vasculitis (HSP) is the most common childhood vasculitis. Around 30 to 50% develop renal involvement, usually within four weeks of onset, ranging from isolated haematuria through to crescentic glomerulonephritis. All affected children require weekly urinalysis and blood pressure measurement for at least 6 weeks. Microscopic haematuria alone is observed. Persistent or significant proteinuria, hypertension or rising creatinine requires urgent paediatric nephrology referral and consideration of biopsy and immunosuppressive therapy. Long-term renal follow-up is required because hypertension and CKD can develop years later.
key_points: 30 to 50% of HSP develop renal involvement, usually within 4 weeks
All require weekly urinalysis and BP for at least 6 weeks
Proteinuria, hypertension or rising creatinine = urgent nephrology referral
Long-term follow-up because hypertension and CKD can develop years later
explanation.pearls: For HSP with urine PCR >250 mg/mmol, hypertension above the 95th centile, or rising creatinine, refer to paediatric nephrology within 48 hours rather than continuing observation, because crescentic disease can progress rapidly to end-stage kidney disease.

stem: A 5-month-old boy is referred to your paediatric inpatient team after a third hospital admission with severe pneumonia, this time with Pneumocystis jirovecii on bronchoalveolar lavage. He had oral candidiasis at 8 weeks, persistent watery diarrhoea since 3 months with documented failure to thrive crossing two major weight centiles downward, and chronic perianal excoriation. His maternal uncle died of overwhelming infection at 7 months. Vaccinations include BCG at birth, with no localised reaction at the BCG site. Lymphocyte counts on previous admissions have been persistently low.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, BP 84/52, RR 56, SpO2 91% on 2 L NP, Temp 38.7
data_table.Examination: Cachectic; oral candidiasis; bibasal crackles; perianal excoriation
data_table.Investigations: Lymphocytes 0.4 (low for age); IgG 1.8 g/L (low); BAL Pneumocystis positive; CXR diffuse interstitial

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence oral itraconazole prophylaxis alone and arrange routine paediatric outpatient follow-up in six weeks without immunology referral or hospital admission for further investigation
options.rationale: Antifungal prophylaxis alone does not address the underlying immunodeficiency; immunology referral and definitive haematopoietic stem cell transplant planning are urgent. (Source: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement)
source_refs: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement

options.text: Continue co-trimoxazole, plan rotavirus catch-up vaccination this admission alongside MMR booster at 6 months and discharge once afebrile and feeding without further investigation
options.rationale: Rotavirus and MMR are live vaccines and are absolutely contraindicated in suspected SCID due to risk of disseminated vaccine-strain infection. (Source: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement)
source_refs: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement

options.text: Transfuse 10 mL per kilogram of non-irradiated packed red cells for an anticipated drop in haemoglobin during this acute respiratory illness without further immunology consultation today
options.rationale: Non-irradiated blood in SCID can cause fatal transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease; only irradiated CMV-negative blood products are used. (Source: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement)
source_refs: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement

options.text: Reassure the family that recurrent infections are normal at this age and continue routine immunisation schedule including BCG booster at 6 months of age in next month
options.rationale: Three serious infections including Pneumocystis pneumonia, failure to thrive, lymphopenia and family history are red flag features for primary immunodeficiency, not normal variation. (Source: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement)
source_refs: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement

options.text: Avoid live vaccines, place in protective isolation, withhold any non-irradiated blood products and refer urgently to a paediatric immunology centre for SCID confirmation and definitive treatment
options.rationale: The clinical and family pattern, lymphopenia, Pneumocystis pneumonia and BCG-without-reaction strongly suggest severe combined immunodeficiency; live vaccines and non-irradiated blood products risk graft-versus-host disease, and definitive treatment is haematopoietic stem cell transplantation. (Source: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement)
source_refs: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement

explanation.summary: Severe combined immunodeficiency presents with severe and recurrent infections (especially Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, persistent thrush and chronic diarrhoea), failure to thrive and lymphopenia for age, often with positive family history of early infant death. BCG given at birth can disseminate without local reaction. Immediate management is protective isolation, avoidance of live vaccines, avoidance of non-irradiated and non-CMV-negative blood products, co-trimoxazole prophylaxis and IVIG support, with urgent referral to a paediatric immunology centre for confirmation by lymphocyte subsets and genetic testing. Haematopoietic stem cell transplantation is curative and is most effective before significant infection burden.
key_points: Red flags: Pneumocystis pneumonia, thrush after 2 months, lymphopenia, family history
BCG can disseminate without local reaction
Avoid live vaccines and non-irradiated blood products
Definitive treatment is haematopoietic stem cell transplantation
explanation.pearls: If a young infant has Pneumocystis pneumonia plus failure to thrive plus absolute lymphocyte count under 1.5 with positive family history, treat as SCID until disproven by placing in isolation, ordering only irradiated CMV-negative blood, withholding live vaccines and contacting paediatric immunology the same day for transplant pathway planning.

sources.label: ASCIA Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Position Statement
BMJ Best Practice: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency
